steps.title: Identify the core of the conclusion
steps.content: The team concluded that anatomical features affect dogs' ability to distinguish speech from nonspeech. This is the precise claim being tested — not language familiarity, not one language from another, but the ability to detect whether a sound is human speech or not. Any supporting finding must link a specific anatomical difference to this speech-versus-nonspeech distinction.

steps.title: Match the finding to the anatomical-speech distinction
steps.content:
Choice B directly shows that dogs with different head shapes (an anatomical feature) differ in how well they separate their brain responses to language recordings from their responses to scrambled nonspeech. Longer-headed dogs show a greater difference between speech and nonspeech; shorter-headed dogs show less. This directly ties anatomy to the ability to detect speech from nonspeech.

The other choices fall short: A only compares brain activity within long-headed dogs across familiar languages, not the speech-nonspeech distinction. C mixes incompatible conditions (scrambled nonspeech with familiar language in different head types). D tests discrimination between two languages, not language from nonspeech. That matches choice B — B is correct.
